title: Psalms 91
subtitle: He Who Dwells in the Shelter of the Most High

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “He Will Cover You with His Feathers”

heb.paragraph: "Whoever dwells in the shelter (sēṭer) of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty" (v.1). THE protection psalm. Four divine names in two verses: Most High (ʿelyōn), Almighty (shadday), LORD (YHWH), God (ʾĕlōhîm). Four images of protection: shelter (v.1), shadow (v.1), refuge/fortress (v.2), feathers/wings (v.4). No fear of night-terror, day-arrow, dark-pestilence, or noon-destruction (vv.5–6). A thousand fall at your side, ten thousand at your right hand, but it will not come near you (v.7). Satan quotes vv.11–12 to tempt Jesus (Matt 4:6).

notes.note: "Whoever dwells in the shelter of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty. I will say of the LORD, ‘He is my refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust.’" Four names, four protections, one trust declaration.

"He will save you from the fowler’s snare and from the deadly pestilence. He will cover you with his feathers, and under his wings you will find refuge." The bird-mother image: God’s wings as shelter. The feathers are soft; the protection is absolute.

"You will not fear the terror of night, nor the arrow that flies by day. A thousand may fall at your side, ten thousand at your right hand, but it will not come near you." Four threats (night, day, dark, noon) matched by total immunity. The numbers escalate: one thousand, ten thousand — still safe.

refs.note: Satan quotes verses 11-12 when tempting Jesus to throw himself from the temple.
The parallel temptation account confirms this psalm's misuse as proof-text for presumption.
Angels as ministering spirits sent to serve heirs of salvation echoes the psalm's angelic protection.

hist.context: Psalm 91 promises comprehensive divine protection: from plague, arrows, terror, lions, and serpents. The four divine names in verses 1-2 (Most High, Almighty, LORD, God) emphasize the protector's identity. Whether this describes an individual's confession or priestly blessing over worshipers is debated. Jesus's temptation shows the psalm can be misapplied.

sections.header: Verses 9–16 — “He Will Command His Angels Concerning You”

heb.paragraph: "He will command his angels (malʾāk̅āyw) concerning you to guard you in all your ways; they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone" (vv.11–12). Satan quotes these exact verses to tempt Jesus to jump from the temple pinnacle (Matt 4:6; Luke 4:10–11). Jesus responds: "Do not put the Lord your God to the test." The promise is real; Satan’s application is twisted. "You will tread on the lion and the cobra" (v.13). God speaks in first person (vv.14–16): "Because he loves me, I will rescue him. He will call on me, and I will answer. I will be with him in trouble. With long life I will satisfy him and show him my salvation."

notes.note: "He will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways." Satan quotes this to Jesus (Matt 4:6). The promise is genuine; the temptation is to presume upon it. Protection is promised to those who dwell, not to those who test.
"You will tread on the lion and the cobra; you will trample the great lion and the serpent." Dominion over predators and poisons. Jesus gives the seventy-two similar authority (Luke 10:19).

"Because he loves me, says the LORD, I will rescue him. He will call on me, and I will answer him; I will be with him in trouble. I will deliver him and honour him. With long life I will satisfy him and show him my salvation." God’s direct speech closes the psalm with seven promises: rescue, protect, answer, accompany, deliver, honour, satisfy.

refs.note: Authority to trample snakes and scorpions echoes the psalm's promise of treading on lions and cobras.
God crushing Satan under believers' feet develops the serpent-trampling imagery.
The ancient serpent identified as Satan connects the psalm's reptilian imagery to cosmic conflict.

hist.context: The divine oracle (vv.14-16) shifts to first-person: 'Because he loves me, I will rescue him.' God personally guarantees deliverance, long life, and salvation. The psalm became associated with protection from demons in Second Temple Judaism. Christian interpretation balances its promises against Job's suffering and Jesus's passion.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: I will say of the LORD, ‘He is my refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIf you say, “The Lord is my refuge,” and you make the Most High your dwelling,ESVBecause you have made the LORD your dwelling place\u2014 the Most High, who is my refuge\u2014NIVHe will cover you with his feathers, and under his wings you will find refuge; his faithfulness will be your shield and rampart.ESVHe will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge; his faithfulness is a shield and buckler.

rec.text: ""Whoever dwells in the shelter of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty. I will say of the LORD, ‘He is my refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust.’" Four names, four protectio…"

rows.label: The Angels — 91:9–16
rows.text: "He will command his ANGELS." Satan quotes this to Jesus (Matt 4:6). Seven divine promises. "I will be with him in trouble."

hebtext.note: "Whoever dwells in the shelter (sēṭer) of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty" (v.1). THE protection psalm. A thousand fall at your side, ten thousand at your right hand, but it will not come near you (v.7). Satan quotes vv.11–12 to tempt Jesus (Matt 4:6).

"He will command his angels (malʾāk̅āyw) concerning you to guard you in all your ways; they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone" (vv.11–12). Satan quotes these exact verses to tempt Jesus to jump from the temple pinnacle (Matt 4:6; Luke 4:10–11). Jesus responds: "Do not put the Lord your God to the test." He will call on me, and I will answer. I will be with him in trouble. With long life I will satisfy him and show him my salvation."

themes.note: Faith peaks: the supreme protection psalm — four names, four protections, seven promises. Prophecy: Satan quotes vv.11–12 at the temptation of Christ. Mercy: feathers, wings, angels, divine presence in trouble.
